question: Explain the operational limitations of the P-T limit curve during normal operation.

nuscale_ch05_0010.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Materials
nuscale_ch05_0010.summary: The Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) materials, including welds, are selected and fabricated in accordance with ASME BPVC standards and meet the regulatory requirements of 10 CFR 50, including General Design Criteria (GDC) for quality, environmental compatibility, and fracture prevention. The materials used for Class 1 components, such as the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) and steam generators (SGs), are specified in Table 5.2-4 and are subject to strict quality assurance and fracture toughness criteria.

nuscale_ch05_0008.title: Compliance with Codes and Code Cases
nuscale_ch05_0008.summary: This section describes how the NuScale Power Modules (NPMs) comply with relevant regulatory requirements, specifically 10 CFR 50.55a and associated General Design Criteria, by designing, fabricating, and testing reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) components to ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) Class 1 standards. It also details the classification of connected piping and confirms that the 2013 Edition of the ASME BPVC is used as the code of record, in accordance with NRC regulations and guidance.

nuscale_ch05_0016.title: Pressure-Temperature Limits, Pressurized Thermal Shock, and Charpy Upper-Shelf Energy Data and Analyses
nuscale_ch05_0016.summary: This section outlines the basis for establishing operational pressure and temperature limits for the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) to comply with regulatory requirements over the plant’s 60-year lifespan. It describes the use of ASME and NRC methodologies to calculate pressure-temperature limit curves, accounting for vessel embrittlement, and provides the relevant figures and tables that define safe operating conditions to prevent non-ductile failure of the reactor vessel.

nuscale_ch05_0015.title: Reactor Vessel Materials
nuscale_ch05_0015.summary: The Reactor Vessel section describes the NuScale Power Module’s reactor pressure vessel (RPV), which houses the core, steam generators, and pressurizer, serving as a key barrier to fission product release and supporting various reactor components. The RPV and its materials are selected and fabricated according to ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code standards to ensure integrity and safety throughout the plant’s design lifetime.

nuscale_ch05_0012.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Leakage Detection
nuscale_ch05_0012.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) does not use traditional components with designed leakage, making it impractical to distinguish between identified and unidentified leakage inside containment using current instrumentation. All leakage into containment is treated as unidentified until the source is found, with leakage rates determined by RCS inventory balance and compared to containment evacuation system (CES) flow rates; the detection systems are reliable and support leak-before-break analyses.

nuscale_ch05_0024.title: Pressurizer
nuscale_ch05_0024.summary: The pressurizer in the NuScale reactor is integrated into the upper region of the reactor vessel and functions to regulate reactor coolant system (RCS) pressure by maintaining a saturated steam-water interface. It uses heaters and spray flow to control pressure, and is designed to handle operating transients without triggering reactor trips or safety system actuations, while providing continuous monitoring and control through instrumentation.

nuscale_ch05_0002.title: Summary Description
nuscale_ch05_0002.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping to transfer heat from the reactor core to the steam generator, ensuring safe and efficient operation. The RCS design supports up to twelve independent modules, maintains proper coolant chemistry and boron concentration, and is engineered to prevent fuel design limit violations during normal and off-normal conditions.

nuscale_ch05_0007.title: Integrity of Reactor Coolant Boundary
nuscale_ch05_0007.summary: This section describes the design features of each NuScale Power Module (NPM) that ensure the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) throughout the plant’s design life. It details the components and systems included in the RCPB, such as the reactor pressure vessel, containment isolation valves, safety valves, and associated piping, all designed to meet regulatory definitions and maintain pressure containment. The section also references related components and further sections for detailed descriptions and evaluations.

nuscale_ch05_0017.title: Reactor Vessel Integrity
nuscale_ch05_0017.summary: This section outlines the procedures and regulatory compliance measures to ensure the integrity of the NuScale reactor vessel over its 60-year design life. It details the development of operating procedures to prevent exceeding pressure-temperature limits, evaluates the vessel’s resistance to pressurized thermal shock and upper-shelf energy degradation, and confirms that all predicted values remain within regulatory safety criteria.

edges.description: Section A describes the material requirements for the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) and references compliance with ASME BPVC and 10 CFR regulations, while Section B specifies that the RCPB components are designed, fabricated, and inspected in accordance with those same ASME BPVC and regulatory requirements, thus Section B provides the general compliance framework that Section A details for materials.

Section A establishes that the reactor coolant pressure boundary materials comply with ASME BPVC and regulatory requirements, which is a prerequisite for the validity and applicability of the P-T limit curve discussed in Section B, as the curve's operational limitations depend on the material properties and their code compliance.

Section A specifies the regulatory and material standards that the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) materials must meet, while Section B describes the reactor vessel as a component constructed from those materials, thus Section A provides the detailed requirements that Section B's general description relies on.

Section A establishes that the reactor pressure vessel and its components comply with ASME BPVC requirements, which are the regulatory basis for the pressure-temperature (P-T) limits discussed in Section B, thereby linking the operational limitations of the P-T curve to code compliance.

Section A establishes that the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) and its components comply with regulatory codes (such as ASME BPVC), while Section B describes the physical configuration and function of the RPV as a pressure-retaining component, thus Section B provides the general context for which Section A specifies compliance details.

Section A specifies the examination and welding requirements for certain reactor vessel attachments, which are constructed from the materials and components described in Section B, thereby linking operational limitations of the P-T limit curve to the physical characteristics and construction of the reactor vessel.

Section B establishes that the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) materials comply with regulatory and ASME code requirements, which is a prerequisite for the operational limitations discussed in Section A regarding leakage detection, as the integrity and properties of these materials directly affect leakage behavior and detection capability.

Section A discusses the practical limitations in detecting reactor coolant pressure boundary leakage, which impacts the ability to ensure operational compliance with the pressure-temperature (P-T) limit curve requirements specified in Section B's referenced codes and regulations.

Section A discusses the inability to distinguish specific sources of reactor coolant pressure boundary leakage with current instrumentation, which impacts the application and enforcement of the operational limitations defined by the P-T limit curve in Section B, as accurate leakage detection is necessary to ensure compliance with those limits during normal operation.

Section A describes the pressurizer's design and function, which directly affects the pressure and temperature conditions that must be maintained within the operational limits defined by the P-T (pressure-temperature) limit curve, while Section B specifies the material requirements and regulatory standards that these operational limits are intended to protect, thus linking the pressurizer's operation to the integrity constraints of the reactor coolant pressure boundary materials.

Section A describes the design and function of the pressurizer, which is a pressure-retaining component whose operational limitations (such as those imposed by the P-T limit curve) must comply with the regulatory requirements and codes specified in Section B.

Section A describes the pressurizer's role in maintaining reactor coolant system pressure, which directly affects the operational envelope defined by the pressure-temperature (P-T) limit curves discussed in Section B, as these curves set regulatory boundaries for safe operation based on pressure and temperature conditions.

Section A describes the components and configuration of the reactor coolant system, which establishes the operational context for the pressure-temperature (P-T) limits, while Section B specifies the material standards and regulatory requirements for the reactor coolant pressure boundary that directly influence the derivation and application of the P-T limit curve during normal operation.

Section A describes the components and design approach of the reactor coolant system, which are subject to the regulatory requirements and codes for integrity and safety compliance detailed in Section B.

Section A describes the overall design and components of the reactor coolant system, which establishes the operational context in which the pressure-temperature (P-T) limit curve (addressed in Section B) must be applied and adhered to during normal operation.

Section B specifies the material standards and regulatory compliance that Section A relies on to ensure the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, which is foundational for establishing operational limitations such as the P-T limit curve during normal operation.

Section A describes the design features that ensure the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, while Section B specifies that these features comply with regulatory codes and standards, thereby connecting the design's operational limitations (such as P-T limit curves) to regulatory compliance.

Section B provides specific pressure-temperature (P-T) limit analyses and requirements that are necessary to ensure the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary described in Section A during normal operation.

Section A describes the development of operating procedures to ensure that pressure-temperature (P-T) limits are not exceeded during normal operation, which is necessary because Section B establishes that the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) materials must comply with ASME and regulatory requirements that underpin those P-T limits.

Section A describes the development of operating procedures to ensure that pressure-temperature (P-T) limits are not exceeded during normal operation, which is necessary to comply with the design and fabrication requirements for reactor pressure vessels specified in Section B.

Section A discusses the development of operating procedures to ensure that pressure-temperature (P-T) limits are not exceeded during normal operation, which directly relies on the P-T limits and analyses described in Section B.

Section A provides specific design details of the pressurizer, which is identified in Section B as a component of the reactor coolant system (RCS) relevant to the operational limitations of the P-T limit curve.

Section A describes the pressurizer's role in maintaining reactor coolant system pressure, which directly supports the operational integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary discussed in Section B, as required for safe operation within P-T limit curves.

Section B establishes the requirement to develop operating procedures that ensure pressure-temperature (P-T) limits are not exceeded during normal operation, while Section A describes the pressurizer's role in maintaining reactor coolant system pressure, which directly affects adherence to those P-T limits.

Section A describes the components and configuration of the reactor coolant system (RCS), which Section B identifies as forming the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) whose integrity is protected by operational limitations such as the P-T limit curve.

Section B specifies that operating procedures are developed to ensure the pressure-temperature (P-T) limits are not exceeded during normal operation, which is a constraint on the RCS described in Section A.

Section B describes the development of operating procedures to ensure that pressure-temperature (P-T) limits are not exceeded during normal operation, which directly supports the integrity of the reactor coolant boundary as required in Section A.
